The {{nihongo|'''Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun'''|綿貫観音山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Watanuki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1973.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162321|title=観音山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th Century, and many of the [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] discovered during archaeological excavation were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Properties]] in 1994<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/135427|title=群馬県舞台一号墳出土品 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> In the year 2020, the collective status of 3346 artifacts from the tomb was raised to that of a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Terazawa |first1=Naoaki |title=群馬）出土品、輝く「国宝」 綿貫観音山古墳展 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN7V7KGTN7VUHNB002.html |publisher=Asahi Shinbun Digital |date=July 27, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun is located on a plain on the west bank of the Ino River, six kilometers east of the city of Takasaki, and is constructed facing north. Its main dimensions are:
;Total length: 97 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 64 meters wide x 9.4 meters high, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 61 meter diameter x 9.6 meters high, 2-tiers
An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted by the Gunma Prefectural Board of Education from 1967 to 1968. The tumulus has a two-tiered construction and a double horseshoe-shaped moat. From the excavated [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, the date of construction is estimated to be the latter half of the 6th century AD. ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, but no trace of ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered. The ''haniwa'' were of especial interest, as they depict men, women, boys and girls, in various costume, including what appear to be warriors in armor and with shields, farmers and aristocrats. There are also house-shaped ''haniwa'' and animal-shaped (horses and chicken) ''haniwa'' as well as ''haniwa'' shaped in the form of equipment and farm implements. Taken as a group, the ''haniwa'' appear to be narrating an event, possibly that of the accession of a new chief.
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is a horizontal trapezoidal stone-lined room in the middle of the posterior circular portion, opening towards the southwest. It was found to be intact. The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] blocks, and the ceiling stones are [[:en:Sandstone|Sandstone]]. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in [[:en:Northern Qi|Northern Qi]], and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in [[:en:Gongju|Gongju]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the [[:en:Keno Province|Keno region]] of ancient Japan and the ancient [[:en:Korea|Korea]].
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.

The {{nihongo|'''Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun'''|綿貫観音山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Watanuki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1973.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162321|title=観音山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th Century, and many of the [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] discovered during archaeological excavation were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Properties]] in 1994<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/135427|title=群馬県舞台一号墳出土品 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> In the year 2020, the collective status of 3346 artifacts from the tomb was raised to that of a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Terazawa |first1=Naoaki |title=群馬）出土品、輝く「国宝」 綿貫観音山古墳展 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN7V7KGTN7VUHNB002.html |publisher=Asahi Shinbun Digital |date=July 27, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun is located on a plain on the west bank of the Ino River, six kilometers east of the city of Takasaki, and is constructed facing north. Its main dimensions are:
;Total length: 97 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 64 meters wide x 9.4 meters high, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 61 meter diameter x 9.6 meters high, 2-tiers
An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted by the Gunma Prefectural Board of Education from 1967 to 1968. The tumulus has a two-tiered construction and a double horseshoe-shaped moat. From the excavated [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, the date of construction is estimated to be the latter half of the 6th century AD. ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, but no trace of ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered. The ''haniwa'' were of especial interest, as they depict men, women, boys and girls, in various costume, including what appear to be warriors in armor and with shields, farmers and aristocrats. There are also house-shaped ''haniwa'' and animal-shaped (horses and chicken) ''haniwa'' as well as ''haniwa'' shaped in the form of equipment and farm implements. Taken as a group, the ''haniwa'' appear to be narrating an event, possibly that of the accession of a new chief.
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is a horizontal trapezoidal stone-lined room in the middle of the posterior circular portion, opening towards the southwest. It was found to be intact. The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] blocks, and the ceiling stones are [[:en:Sandstone|Sandstone]]. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in [[:en:Northern Qi|Northern Qi]], and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in [[:en:Gongju|Gongju]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the [[:en:Keno Province|Keno region]] of ancient Japan and the ancient [[:en:Korea|Korea]].
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.

The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1973.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162321|title=観音山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th Century, and many of the [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] discovered during archaeological excavation were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Properties]] in 1994<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/135427|title=群馬県舞台一号墳出土品 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> In the year 2020, the collective status of 3346 artifacts from the tomb was raised to that of a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Terazawa |first1=Naoaki |title=群馬）出土品、輝く「国宝」 綿貫観音山古墳展 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN7V7KGTN7VUHNB002.html |publisher=Asahi Shinbun Digital |date=July 27, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun is located on a plain on the west bank of the Ino River, six kilometers east of the city of Takasaki, and is constructed facing north. Its main dimensions are:
;Total length: 97 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 64 meters wide x 9.4 meters high, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 61 meter diameter x 9.6 meters high, 2-tiers
An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted by the Gunma Prefectural Board of Education from 1967 to 1968. The tumulus has a two-tiered construction and a double horseshoe-shaped moat. From the excavated [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, the date of construction is estimated to be the latter half of the 6th century AD. ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, but no trace of ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered. The ''haniwa'' were of especial interest, as they depict men, women, boys and girls, in various costume, including what appear to be warriors in armor and with shields, farmers and aristocrats. There are also house-shaped ''haniwa'' and animal-shaped (horses and chicken) ''haniwa'' as well as ''haniwa'' shaped in the form of equipment and farm implements. Taken as a group, the ''haniwa'' appear to be narrating an event, possibly that of the accession of a new chief.
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is a horizontal trapezoidal stone-lined room in the middle of the posterior circular portion, opening towards the southwest. It was found to be intact. The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]] blocks, and the ceiling stones are [[:en:Sandstone|Sandstone]]. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in [[:en:Northern Qi|Northern Qi]], and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]] of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in [[:en:Gongju|Gongju]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the [[:en:Keno Province|Keno region]] of ancient Japan and the ancient [[:en:Korea|Korea]].
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance to burial chamber File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, sekishitsu.jpg|Inside burial chamber File:Watanuki-kannon-stereo.jpg|Stereoscopic view from above File:Haniwa - Warrior in Keiko Armor (Watanuki Kannon) 01.jpg|Watanuki Kannonyama Haniwa - designated National Treasure in 2020 </gallery>

The {{nihongo|'''Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun'''|綿貫観音山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Watanuki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1973.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162321|title=観音山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th Century, and many of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} discovered during archaeological excavation were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Properties}} in 1994<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/135427|title=群馬県舞台一号墳出土品 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> In the year 2020, the collective status of 3346 artifacts from the tomb was raised to that of a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Terazawa |first1=Naoaki |title=群馬）出土品、輝く「国宝」 綿貫観音山古墳展 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN7V7KGTN7VUHNB002.html |publisher=Asahi Shinbun Digital |date=July 27, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun is located on a plain on the west bank of the Ino River, six kilometers east of the city of Takasaki, and is constructed facing north. Its main dimensions are:
;Total length: 97 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 64 meters wide x 9.4 meters high, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 61 meter diameter x 9.6 meters high, 2-tiers
An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted by the Gunma Prefectural Board of Education from 1967 to 1968. The tumulus has a two-tiered construction and a double horseshoe-shaped moat. From the excavated {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery, the date of construction is estimated to be the latter half of the 6th century AD. ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, but no trace of ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered. The ''haniwa'' were of especial interest, as they depict men, women, boys and girls, in various costume, including what appear to be warriors in armor and with shields, farmers and aristocrats. There are also house-shaped ''haniwa'' and animal-shaped (horses and chicken) ''haniwa'' as well as ''haniwa'' shaped in the form of equipment and farm implements. Taken as a group, the ''haniwa'' appear to be narrating an event, possibly that of the accession of a new chief.
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is a horizontal trapezoidal stone-lined room in the middle of the posterior circular portion, opening towards the southwest. It was found to be intact. The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} blocks, and the ceiling stones are {{ill|Sandstone|en|Sandstone|WD=Q13085|lt=Sandstone}}. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}}, copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in {{ill|Northern Qi|en|Northern Qi|WD=Q1143524|lt=Northern Qi}}, and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in {{ill|Gongju|en|Gongju|WD=Q42062|lt=Gongju}}, {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|WD=Q884|lt=South Korea}}. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno region}} of ancient Japan and the ancient {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}}.
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.

{{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=red|text align=center|[[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun'''|綿貫観音山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Watanuki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1973.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162321|title=観音山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th Century, and many of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} discovered during archaeological excavation were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Properties}} in 1994<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/135427|title=群馬県舞台一号墳出土品 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> In the year 2020, the collective status of 3346 artifacts from the tomb was raised to that of a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Terazawa |first1=Naoaki |title=群馬）出土品、輝く「国宝」 綿貫観音山古墳展 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN7V7KGTN7VUHNB002.html |publisher=Asahi Shinbun Digital |date=July 27, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun is located on a plain on the west bank of the Ino River, six kilometers east of the city of Takasaki, and is constructed facing north. Its main dimensions are:
;Total length: 97 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 64 meters wide x 9.4 meters high, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 61 meter diameter x 9.6 meters high, 2-tiers
An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted by the Gunma Prefectural Board of Education from 1967 to 1968. The tumulus has a two-tiered construction and a double horseshoe-shaped moat. From the excavated {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery, the date of construction is estimated to be the latter half of the 6th century AD. ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, but no trace of ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered. The ''haniwa'' were of especial interest, as they depict men, women, boys and girls, in various costume, including what appear to be warriors in armor and with shields, farmers and aristocrats. There are also house-shaped ''haniwa'' and animal-shaped (horses and chicken) ''haniwa'' as well as ''haniwa'' shaped in the form of equipment and farm implements. Taken as a group, the ''haniwa'' appear to be narrating an event, possibly that of the accession of a new chief.
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is a horizontal trapezoidal stone-lined room in the middle of the posterior circular portion, opening towards the southwest. It was found to be intact. The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} blocks, and the ceiling stones are {{ill|Sandstone|en|Sandstone|WD=Q13085|lt=Sandstone}}. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}}, copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in {{ill|Northern Qi|en|Northern Qi|WD=Q1143524|lt=Northern Qi}}, and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in {{ill|Gongju|en|Gongju|WD=Q42062|lt=Gongju}}, {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|WD=Q884|lt=South Korea}}. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno region}} of ancient Japan and the ancient {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}}.
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance to burial chamber File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, sekishitsu.jpg|Inside burial chamber File:Watanuki-kannon-stereo.jpg|Stereoscopic view from above File:Haniwa - Warrior in Keiko Armor (Watanuki Kannon) 01.jpg|Watanuki Kannonyama Haniwa - designated National Treasure in 2020 </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|WD=Q6591583|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)}}

{{Short description|Kofun in Takasaki, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun | native_name =綿貫観音山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.307222222222|N|139.07722222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|1=Takasaki}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Museum at site) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=red|text align=center|[[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun'''|綿貫観音山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Watanuki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1973.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162321|title=観音山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th Century, and many of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} discovered during archaeological excavation were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1994<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/135427|title=群馬県舞台一号墳出土品 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> In the year 2020, the collective status of 3346 artifacts from the tomb was raised to that of a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Terazawa |first1=Naoaki |title=群馬）出土品、輝く「国宝」 綿貫観音山古墳展 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN7V7KGTN7VUHNB002.html |publisher=Asahi Shinbun Digital |date=July 27, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun is located on a plain on the west bank of the Ino River, six kilometers east of the city of Takasaki, and is constructed facing north. Its main dimensions are:
;Total length: 97 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 64 meters wide x 9.4 meters high, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 61 meter diameter x 9.6 meters high, 2-tiers
An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted by the Gunma Prefectural Board of Education from 1967 to 1968. The tumulus has a two-tiered construction and a double horseshoe-shaped moat. From the excavated {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery, the date of construction is estimated to be the latter half of the 6th century AD. ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, but no trace of ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered. The ''haniwa'' were of especial interest, as they depict men, women, boys and girls, in various costume, including what appear to be warriors in armor and with shields, farmers and aristocrats. There are also house-shaped ''haniwa'' and animal-shaped (horses and chicken) ''haniwa'' as well as ''haniwa'' shaped in the form of equipment and farm implements. Taken as a group, the ''haniwa'' appear to be narrating an event, possibly that of the accession of a new chief.
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is a horizontal trapezoidal stone-lined room in the middle of the posterior circular portion, opening towards the southwest. It was found to be intact. The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} blocks, and the ceiling stones are {{ill|Sandstone|en|Sandstone|WD=Q13085|lt=Sandstone|1=Sandstone}}. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}}, copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in {{ill|Northern Qi|en|Northern Qi|WD=Q1143524|lt=Northern Qi|1=Northern Qi}}, and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong|1=Tomb of King Muryeong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in {{ill|Gongju|en|Gongju|WD=Q42062|lt=Gongju|1=Gongju}}, {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|WD=Q884|lt=South Korea|1=South Korea}}. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno region|1=Keno Province}} of ancient Japan and the ancient {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}.
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance to burial chamber File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, sekishitsu.jpg|Inside burial chamber File:Watanuki-kannon-stereo.jpg|Stereoscopic view from above File:Haniwa - Warrior in Keiko Armor (Watanuki Kannon) 01.jpg|Watanuki Kannonyama Haniwa - designated National Treasure in 2020 </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|WD=Q6591583|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun]][[en:Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun]][[ja:綿貫観音山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun]][[en:Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun]][[ja:綿貫観音山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun]][[en:Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun]][[ja:綿貫観音山古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun in Takasaki, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun | native_name =綿貫観音山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.307222222222|N|139.07722222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|1=Takasaki}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Museum at site) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=red|text align=center|[[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Watanuke Kannonyama Kofun'''|綿貫観音山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Watanuki neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1973.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162321|title=観音山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th Century, and many of the {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} discovered during archaeological excavation were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1994<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/135427|title=群馬県舞台一号墳出土品 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> In the year 2020, the collective status of 3346 artifacts from the tomb was raised to that of a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|National Treasure]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Terazawa |first1=Naoaki |title=群馬）出土品、輝く「国宝」 綿貫観音山古墳展 |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN7V7KGTN7VUHNB002.html |publisher=Asahi Shinbun Digital |date=July 27, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Watanuki Kannonyama Kofun is located on a plain on the west bank of the Ino River, six kilometers east of the city of Takasaki, and is constructed facing north. Its main dimensions are:
;Total length: 97 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 64 meters wide x 9.4 meters high, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 61 meter diameter x 9.6 meters high, 2-tiers
An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted by the Gunma Prefectural Board of Education from 1967 to 1968. The tumulus has a two-tiered construction and a double horseshoe-shaped moat. From the excavated {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery, the date of construction is estimated to be the latter half of the 6th century AD. ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, but no trace of ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered. The ''haniwa'' were of especial interest, as they depict men, women, boys and girls, in various costume, including what appear to be warriors in armor and with shields, farmers and aristocrats. There are also house-shaped ''haniwa'' and animal-shaped (horses and chicken) ''haniwa'' as well as ''haniwa'' shaped in the form of equipment and farm implements. Taken as a group, the ''haniwa'' appear to be narrating an event, possibly that of the accession of a new chief.
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is a horizontal trapezoidal stone-lined room in the middle of the posterior circular portion, opening towards the southwest. It was found to be intact. The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} blocks, and the ceiling stones are {{ill|Sandstone|en|Sandstone|WD=Q13085|lt=Sandstone|1=Sandstone}}. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}}, copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in {{ill|Northern Qi|en|Northern Qi|WD=Q1143524|lt=Northern Qi|1=Northern Qi}}, and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong|1=Tomb of King Muryeong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in {{ill|Gongju|en|Gongju|WD=Q42062|lt=Gongju|1=Gongju}}, {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|WD=Q884|lt=South Korea|1=South Korea}}. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno region|1=Keno Province}} of ancient Japan and the ancient {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}.
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance to burial chamber File:Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun, sekishitsu.jpg|Inside burial chamber File:Watanuki-kannon-stereo.jpg|Stereoscopic view from above File:Haniwa - Warrior in Keiko Armor (Watanuki Kannon) 01.jpg|Watanuki Kannonyama Haniwa - designated National Treasure in 2020 </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|WD=Q6591583|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]]{{template-inline|Watanuki Kannon-yama Kofun}} *[https://www.pref.gunma.jp/03/x4510004.html Gunma Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121600309/ Takasaki City official site] {{in lang|ja}}

The dimensions were the largest found in Gunma Prefecture, with a total length of 12.65 meters. The wall stones are {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} blocks, and the ceiling stones are {{ill|Sandstone|en|Sandstone|WD=Q13085|lt=Sandstone|1=Sandstone}}. The blocks weigh up to 22 tons, but as there is no source for these materials from the neighborhood of the Kofun, it was necessary for these stones to be transported from a considerable distance to construct this tomb.
The grave goods included two [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirrors]], gold, silver and glass jewelry, iron swords, iron spearheads, fragments of armor, horse harnesses, saddles, stirrups and Sue pottery, {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}}, copper water bottles and other containers, many of which were found to be in excellent preservation. These included copper water jars and iron helmets thought have been made in {{ill|Northern Qi|en|Northern Qi|WD=Q1143524|lt=Northern Qi|1=Northern Qi}}, and a copper water bottle and a mirror that were identical to objects excavated from the burial chamber of the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong|1=Tomb of King Muryeong}} of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in {{ill|Gongju|en|Gongju|WD=Q42062|lt=Gongju|1=Gongju}}, {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|WD=Q884|lt=South Korea|1=South Korea}}. As these objects must have been cast from the same mould, it indicates a strong connection between the rulers of the {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|WD=Q1886428|lt=Keno region|1=Keno Province}} of ancient Japan and the ancient {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}.
The site was opened to the public as the first historic park in the prefecture in 1981. Some of the excavated items are on display at the Gunma Prefectural Historical Museum.
